entries.title: Tap a screen to see everything on it
entries.detail: Click or tap a camera screen (anywhere that isn't a followable object) and a panel lists every object on that screen, grouped by category. Hovering a row outlines the object on the map, and clicking it jumps straight there. On phones, where there's no hover to inspect with, this is the way to see what a screen holds.

entries.title: Embed the map in wikis and forums
entries.detail: Adding ?embed=1 to the address gives a view made to sit in an iframe: the map fills the frame, still fully interactive, with the sidebar starting tucked away behind its menu button. Pair it with any permalink and the embed opens exactly on the screen you mean; a corner button takes visitors to the full site at the very spot they're looking at.

entries.title: More keyboard shortcuts, and ? to see them all
entries.detail: Press [ and ] to step through the current level's paths, and g, c and f flip the camera grid, collision lines and foreground masks without a trip to the sidebar. Press ? for a card listing every shortcut. Arrows still pan, + and - zoom, / jumps to search.

entries.title: Copy a link to the view you're looking at
entries.detail: The chain button in the top-right corner puts a permalink to the exact spot on screen on your clipboard. The address bar has always carried this link, but on phones (especially with the map opened as an installed app) there may be no address bar to copy from.

entries.title: Screens you've visited can load instantly on your next visit
entries.detail: Turn on "Cache screen artwork on this device" in Settings and visited screens stay stored locally (up to about 150 MB), so returning to the map doesn't re-download them screen by screen. Previously the whole map re-fetched after every site update. Off by default; switching it off removes everything stored.

entries.title: Express wells land on the arrival well
entries.detail: Following an express well now drops the highlight ring on the exact well you pop out of (matched by the same well id the game reads on arrival) instead of the middle of the destination screen. Wells whose switch-off state just bounces you back out now follow their real ride, including local rides to another screen of the same path.

entries.title: One-click reset for Display and Objects
entries.detail: A small reset in the Display and Objects headers puts that section back to its defaults, handy now that view options carry over between visits.

entries.title: Full game, level and path names on the buttons
entries.detail: Turn on "Show full names" in Settings and the game, level and path buttons expand into a list carrying their full names next to the short codes: "AO (Abe's Oddysee)", "R1 (Rupture Farms)", and the named path areas like the Rupture Farms Return zulags and the Exoddus enders.

entries.title: Pick up where you left off
entries.detail: Turning on "Remember last location" in Settings and reopening the site brings you back to the exact game, level, path and view you last had on screen. Opening a shared permalink still goes to the linked spot.

entries.title: Settings panel: view options are remembered
entries.detail: The gear button at the top of the sidebar opens the new Settings panel. The Display toggles and Objects filters now carry over between visits by default (except the ruler, which always starts off); switch off "Remember display & object filters" in settings to start from a clean slate each time.

entries.title: Follow markers land on the destination door
entries.detail: About 250 door and teleporter pairs share the placeholder pair number 0, and following one used to drop the highlight ring at the center of the destination screen. The ring now lands on the 0-numbered partner door there, which is the same door the game itself picks on arrival.

entries.title: Path buttons name their area
entries.detail: Hover over a path button to see the in-game name of the area where the game defines one: Rupture Farms Return's paths show their Zulag (1-4, straight from the save-name table on the disc), and Exoddus endgame paths show their ender area, like Mudanchee Vault Ender or Bonewerkz Ender.

entries.title: Endgame doors and teleporters now follow correctly
entries.detail: In Exoddus, doors, wells and teleporters leading into a level's endgame revisit (the vault trials and the Feeco, Barracks and Bonewerkz enders) pointed at nothing on the map and couldn't be followed. They now land where they should.

entries.title: What's New keeps you posted
entries.detail: The newspaper button in the top-right corner opens this very feed of updates; a dot on it marks entries added since you last looked.

entries.title: One map for every version of the games
entries.detail: The map is built from the PS1 discs, but level layouts and object placements are identical in the PC releases too, so the site no longer leads with PS1 in its titles.

entries.title: Follow hand stones to the view they show
entries.detail: Click a hand stone to jump straight to the camera it reveals, the same way doors, portals and wells follow to their destination.

entries.title: Right-click to copy a link to any object
entries.detail: Right-click any door, portal or object to copy a link that reopens the map right on it, with the spot highlighted when the link is followed.

entries.title: Travel portals land you on the exit
entries.detail: Following a bird portal now drops you exactly on the destination exit rather than the middle of the screen.

entries.title: Explore with the keyboard
entries.detail: Arrow keys pan the map and +/- zoom; the object filters are fully keyboard-operable too.

entries.title: Send feedback from the sidebar
entries.detail: A link in the sidebar footer opens a pre-filled email that carries a link to exactly what you're looking at.

entries.title: Smoother touch controls
entries.detail: Fixed a blue flash when tapping the map and a pinch-zoom glitch that could fling the view off into the distance.

entries.title: Doors that loop back are labelled
entries.detail: Doors and teleporters that lead back to themselves now say so in the tooltip, instead of offering a jump that goes nowhere.

entries.title: Collapse the sidebar on any screen
entries.detail: Tuck the sidebar away to give the map the whole window, at any size.

entries.title: Made for mobile
entries.detail: Pan and pinch-zoom by touch, with the sidebar tucked into a slide-out menu on small screens.

entries.title: Abe's Exoddus joins the map
entries.detail: The entire second game (every level, camera and object) sits alongside Abe's Oddysee. Switch between them with the game tabs.

entries.title: Smarter search
entries.detail: Search results are ranked by relevance, grouped by where they live, and scoped to a level or the whole game. All keyboard-navigable.

entries.title: Follow doors, portals and wells
entries.detail: Click a door, bird portal or express well to jump straight to where it leads.

entries.title: Search every object in the game
entries.detail: Find any Mudokon, switch or object by name and jump to it instantly.

entries.title: Link to any view
entries.detail: The URL keeps your exact position and zoom, so you can bookmark or share a precise spot on the map.

entries.title: Oddworld Map is live
entries.detail: An interactive map of Abe's Oddysee, built straight from the PS1 disc: every camera screen, object and collision line. Pan, zoom, measure with the ruler, export the view and toggle foreground masks.
